unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4! In this unit you will look up at the big sky and learn about the sun, the moon, the stars and the clouds. You will find out why we have day and night, and how the sky changes in the morning, at midday and in the evening. You will watch your own shadow grow long and short as the sun moves. You will learn describing words for the sky and weather, and you will wonder about the world, just like the children in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher audiovisual lecture and learn a few vocabulary words each day.
Complete all six grammar practices, moving from guided examples to independent use.
Listen to the Unit readings, including The Night Samira Counted the Stars, and answer the comprehension questions.
Complete all six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question quiz and student self-assessment.

readings.title: A Poem: “My Shadow”
readings.passageScript:
Read this poem slowly, then say it out loud:
I have a little shadow that goes in and out with me,
And what can be the use of him is more than I can see.
He is very, very like me from the heels up to the head,
And I see him jump before me, when I jump into my bed.
(by Robert Louis Stevenson)

readings.title: Telling Time with a Shadow
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
A sundial is like a clock. A clock uses hands to point at the numbers. A sundial uses a shadow. The shadow moves as the sun moves across the sky. In the morning, the shadow is long. At midday, the shadow is short. In the evening, the shadow is long again. Long ago, people used sundials to tell the time, before they had clocks.

readings.title: Samira's Shadow
readings.passageScript:
Listen to Samira. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“My name is Samira. My shadow changes during the day. In the morning, the sun is low, and my shadow is very long. At midday, the sun is high, and my shadow is short — it is right under my feet! In the evening, the sun is low again, and my shadow is long again.”

readings.title: Why We Have Day and Night
readings.passageScript:
Listen and follow along:
“The sun shines on our planet, Earth. Earth turns slowly, round and round. When your part of Earth faces the sun, it is day. When your part turns away from the sun, it is night. That is why the sun seems to rise in the morning and set in the evening.”

readings.title: The Night Samira Counted the Stars
readings.genre: A warm family story with dialogue.
readings.theme: The sky through one day — sun, shadows, clouds, moon and stars.
readings.setting: A small town in Somalia, from morning until night.
readings.passageScript:
In a small town near the sea, Samira woke up early. The morning sky was pale and pink. The sun was low, and it was climbing slowly into the big sky.
Samira ran outside to feed the goats. On the sandy ground she saw a long, thin shape moving beside her.
“Look, Ishmael!” she called to her big brother. “My shadow is so long this morning!”
Isaac smiled. “In the morning the sun is low,” he said. “When the sun is low, your shadow is long. Watch what happens at midday.”
By midday the sun was high and bright. Samira stood very still and looked down. Her shadow was now short — it hid right under her feet!
“It shrank!” she laughed. “Where did my long shadow go?”
“The sun is high now, so your shadow is short,” said Ishmael. “The sun did not move by itself. Our Earth is turning, round and round, all day long.”
Samira thought about that. The whole Earth was turning, and she could not even feel it. She looked up. A few white clouds floated across the blue sky. One cloud drifted over the sun, and for a moment her shadow disappeared.
“The cloud blocked the light!” said Samira. “No light, no shadow.”
“Now you understand shadows,” said Ishmael.
In the evening, the sky turned orange and red. The sun was low again, and Samira's shadow grew long once more. Their grandmother, Ayeeyo Selma, sat outside on a mat.
“Come, Samira,” she said softly. “The day is leaving, and the night is coming. Sit with me.”
Slowly the sky grew dark. One by one, tiny lights began to shine. Samira had never really looked before.
“Ayeeyo,” she whispered, “there are so many stars!”
“More than anyone can count,” said Ayeeyo Selma. “Each star is like our sun, but very, very far away. Our sun is only a star that is close to us.”
Samira tried to count anyway. “One, two, three, four…” But every time she looked, she found more. She reached fifty and gave up, laughing.
Then the moon rose over the hills, round and bright.
“Why does the moon shine, Ayeeyo?” Samira asked. “Does it have its own light, like the sun?”
“No, little one,” said her grandmother. “The moon has no light of its own. The sun shines on it, and the moon gives that light back to us in the night. Is it not beautiful?”
Samira nodded. She thought about the whole big sky — the sun that made her shadow long and short, the clouds that floated by, the countless stars, and the gentle moon.
“Ayeeyo,” she said, “the sky is full of wonderful things. I never really looked up before.”
Ayeeyo Selma smiled and held her hand. “Good people wonder about the world,” she said. “They look, and they ask, and they say thank you for all this beauty. Tomorrow the sun will rise again, and a new day will begin.”
That night Samira fell asleep happy. In her dreams the stars were still shining, and she was still counting them, one by one, across the big, big sky.

The whole Earth was turning, and she could not even feel it. “One, two, three, four…” But every time she looked, she found more. “Each star is like our sun, but very, very far away.

grammar.title: Describing Words (Adjectives)
grammar.explanation: An adjective is a describing word. It tells us more about a noun — a person, place or thing. Adjectives help us paint a clear picture of the sky and our shadows.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For shadows: long / short — “My shadow is long in the morning.”
For the sun: high / low — “The sun is high at midday.”
For light: bright / dark — “The day is bright. The night is dark.”
For the sky: blue / grey / orange — “The evening sky is orange.”
The adjective usually comes just before the noun, or after the words is/are: “a dark night”, “The night is dark.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The sky is colour blue.”  ✗
Fix: We do not need the word “colour”. Just say the adjective: “The sky is blue.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: An adjective answers “What is it like?” If your sentence sounds plain, add a describing word to make a picture in the reader's mind.
grammar.practice:
Choose a good adjective to finish each sentence (use the words above):
At midday, the sun is ______ in the sky.
In the morning, my shadow is ______.
At night, the sky is ______.
On a clear day, the sky is ______.

grammar.title: Describing Words (Adjectives): Use It
grammar.explanation: An adjective is a describing word. It tells us more about a noun — a person, place or thing. Adjectives help us paint a clear picture of the sky and our shadows. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 4 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For shadows: long / short — “My shadow is long in the morning.”
For the sun: high / low — “The sun is high at midday.”
For light: bright / dark — “The day is bright. The night is dark.”
For the sky: blue / grey / orange — “The evening sky is orange.”
The adjective usually comes just before the noun, or after the words is/are: “a dark night”, “The night is dark.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The sky is colour blue.”  ✗
Fix: We do not need the word “colour”. Just say the adjective: “The sky is blue.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: An adjective answers “What is it like?” If your sentence sounds plain, add a describing word to make a picture in the reader's mind.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: Present Simple (True Facts)
grammar.explanation: We use the present simple to talk about facts and things that are always true. The sky is full of facts, so this tense is perfect for talking about it!
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the plain verb with: I, you, we, they.
I see the moon.
We look at the sky.
They make shadows.
Add -s (or -es) with: he, she, it. The sun, the moon and a shadow are all “it”.
The sun rises in the east.
A shadow forms when light is blocked.
The Earth turns slowly.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “The sun rise in the east.”  ✗    Fix: “The sun rises in the east.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: he, she, it → add the “s”. The sun is an “it”, so it needs the s: “The sun shines.”
grammar.practice:
Write the verb in the present simple:
The sun ______ (rise) in the east.
Shadows ______ (change) during the day.
The moon ______ (shine) at night.
The Earth ______ (turn) round and round.
We ______ (see) stars in the night sky.

grammar.title: Present Simple (True Facts): Use It
grammar.explanation: We use the present simple to talk about facts and things that are always true. The sky is full of facts, so this tense is perfect for talking about it! Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 4 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the plain verb with: I, you, we, they.
I see the moon.
We look at the sky.
They make shadows.
Add -s (or -es) with: he, she, it. The sun, the moon and a shadow are all “it”.
The sun rises in the east.
A shadow forms when light is blocked.
The Earth turns slowly.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “The sun rise in the east.”  ✗    Fix: “The sun rises in the east.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: he, she, it → add the “s”. The sun is an “it”, so it needs the s: “The sun shines.”
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: Past Simple (verbs ending in -ed)
grammar.explanation: We use the past simple to tell what already happened — yesterday, last night, or last week. Many verbs make the past simple by adding -ed.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Add -ed to most verbs to talk about the past:
look → looked:  “Last night I looked at the stars.”
watch → watched:  “We watched the sun set.”
play → played:  “Yesterday I played in the sunshine.”
It stays the same for everyone — I, you, he, she, we, they all use looked.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Yesterday I look at the moon.”  ✗
Fix: For the past, add -ed: “Yesterday I looked at the moon.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: If it already happened, listen for the -ed at the end: “played”, “watched”, “counted”.
grammar.practice:
Write the verb in the past simple (add -ed):
Last night we ______ (look) at the moon.
Yesterday the children ______ (watch) the clouds.
This morning I ______ (play) outside in the sun.
Samira ______ (count) the stars last night.

grammar.title: Past Simple (verbs ending in -ed): Use It
grammar.explanation: We use the past simple to tell what already happened — yesterday, last night, or last week. Many verbs make the past simple by adding -ed. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 4 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Add -ed to most verbs to talk about the past:
look → looked:  “Last night I looked at the stars.”
watch → watched:  “We watched the sun set.”
play → played:  “Yesterday I played in the sunshine.”
It stays the same for everyone — I, you, he, she, we, they all use looked.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Yesterday I look at the moon.”  ✗
Fix: For the past, add -ed: “Yesterday I looked at the moon.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: If it already happened, listen for the -ed at the end: “played”, “watched”, “counted”.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Describe the Sky
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look out of your window right now. Get ready to say:
“It is the ______ (morning / midday / evening).”
“The sky is ______ (blue / grey / orange).”
“The sun is ______ (high / low), and my shadow is ______ (long / short).”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say the time of day? Did you name a sky colour? Did you say where the sun is?

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Explain How a Shadow Forms
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise saying:
“A shadow forms when something blocks the light.”
“The shadow is on the dark side, away from the sun.”
“Shadows are dark because light cannot pass through.”

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Shadow Play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play near a sunny window or lamp with your AI tutor or your family:
Make a shape with your hands. Say: “I am making a ______ shadow.”
Move your hands. Say: “When I move, the shadow moves too!”
Ask the other person to guess your shadow shape. Then swap turns.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Sky Watch Report
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Describe the sky in the morning, at midday and in the evening using precise adjectives.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Name what you see and describe its colour, light or position.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Shadow Scientist
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain how a shadow forms and compare a morning shadow with a midday shadow.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use long, short, high, low, bright and dark where useful.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - Weather Presenter
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Give today's weather report and one simple prediction for later.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Speak in complete sentences and support the report with what you observe.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 1 — Describe Your Shadow
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Go outside on a sunny day and look at your shadow. Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
My shadow is ______ (long / short / dark).
My shadow moves when I ______.
My shadow is on the ______ side of me.
I like my shadow because ______.
writing.modelText: The sky is bright this morning. My shadow is long when the sun is low. At night, I can see the moon and stars.
writing.sentenceStarter: The sky is bright this morning.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 2 — A Sky Journal
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Look at the sky three times in one day. For each time, write about what you see:
In the morning, the sky is ______ and the sun is ______.
At midday, the sky is ______ and my shadow is ______.
In the evening, the sky is ______ and the sun is ______.
Then draw a picture of the sky at each time of day.
writing.modelText:
Morning: The sky is bright and my shadow is long.
Midday: The sun is high and my shadow is short.
Evening: The sky is orange and the sun is low.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Story response: The Night Samira Counted the Stars
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences about the sky today. Start with: “Today the sky is ______.”
Go outside tonight (or remember a night sky). Write two sentences about the moon and the stars.
Draw Samira looking up at the stars with her grandmother. Write one sentence under your picture.
writing.modelText: Samira watched her shadow change during the day. At night, she counted the stars and saw the bright moon. She learned that light helps to make a shadow.
writing.sentenceStarter: Samira watched her shadow change during the day.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 4 - My Sky Journal
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write one observation for morning, midday and evening, then compare the sky at two times.
writing.modelText: In the morning, the sky was pale and bright. At midday, the sun was high and my shadow was short.
writing.sentenceStarter: In the morning, the sky was pale and bright
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 5 - How a Shadow Forms
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write five clear sentences explaining light, an object and the shadow behind it.
writing.modelText: Light travels from the sun. When an object blocks the light, a shadow forms on the other side.
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 6 - A Night-Sky Description
writing.promptAndInstructions: Describe the night sky in six sentences using at least four adjectives.
writing.modelText: The dark sky was wide and quiet. Bright stars shone above the houses, and the moon looked round.
writing.sentenceStarter: The dark sky was wide and quiet
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation

activities.title: Activity 1 — True or False (Sky Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
The sun rises in the east and sets in the west.
The sun is highest in the sky at midnight.
The moon shines because the sun's light falls on it.
Clouds can block the sunlight so shadows do not form.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True   2. False (the sun is highest at midday)   3. True   4. True

activities.title: Activity 2 — Match the Time to the Shadow
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each time of day to the right shadow. Draw two columns and check with your AI tutor.
Morning  →  the sun is low; the shadow is long.
Midday  →  the sun is high; the shadow is short.
Evening  →  the sun is low again; the shadow is long.

activities.title: Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Right now the weather is ______ (sunny / cloudy / windy / rainy).
We see the ______ and the ______ in the sky at night.
At midday my shadow is ______ (long / short).
The sky looks ______ (colour) right now.

activities.title: Activity 4 - Shadow Measure
activities.instructionsAndItems: Measure one shadow in the morning and at midday. Record which is longer and explain why.
activities.answerSummary: Answers vary. Require complete, relevant work that follows every instruction and uses the target Unit language accurately.

quizzes.question: What does 'shadow' mean?
quizzes.options: The place where something comes from. | A dark shape made when something blocks the light. | The brightness that lets us see things. | The star that gives Earth light and heat in the day.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A dark shape made when something blocks the light.
quizzes.explanation: My shadow follows me when the sun shines.

quizzes.question: Which word means: The brightness that lets us see things.
quizzes.explanation: We need light to see things clearly.

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'sun'?
quizzes.explanation: The sun rises in the east and sets in the west.

quizzes.question: The sun is a ____ of light and heat.
quizzes.explanation: The sun is a source of light and heat.

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: When you block the light, you make a shadow.

quizzes.question: Which word means: The space above the Earth that you see when you look up.
quizzes.explanation: sky: The space above the Earth that you see when you look up. The sky is blue in the day and dark at night.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which word means: The early part of the day, after the sun comes up.
quizzes.explanation: morning: The early part of the day, after the sun comes up. In the morning we eat breakfast.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'midday'?
quizzes.explanation: midday: The middle of the day, when the sun is highest. At midday my shadow is very short.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: In the ____ the sky turns orange.
quizzes.explanation: evening: The part of the day before night, when the sun sets. In the evening the sky turns orange.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: sunrise: The time when the sun comes up in the morning. The sky is red at sunrise and at sunset.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

teacherNotes.note:
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the shadow poem with me and explain it?” “Why do we have day and night? Can you explain it in an easy way?” “What happens to my shadow when there are clouds?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you help me practise describing the sky?” “What words can I use for sky colours and weather?” “Can you check if my explanation of shadows is correct?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check if my sky journal sentences are correct?” “Can you help me use more describing words?”
Story: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?” “Can you check my sentences about the sky?” “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?”
Grammar: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check my grammar answers?” “Why do we say ‘the sun rises’ but ‘I rise’?” “Can you give me five more sentences to practise the past simple?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Why do shadows change during the day?” “Can you explain why shadows are dark?” “What happens to my shadow when there are clouds?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Why does the sky change colour?” “Why is the sky usually blue in the day?” “What makes the sky look red or orange at sunset?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Why do we have day and night?” “Why does the moon look different on different nights?” “Is the sun a star? Can you tell me about stars?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “What other adjectives can I use to describe the sky?” “Can you check if my describing sentences are correct?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you quiz me on the weather words?” “Can you help me find more compound words with ‘sun’ or ‘rain’?”

Samira's shadow changes because the sun's position in the sky changes during the day.
Marking guidance: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: When your part of Earth faces the sun, it is day. When your part turns away from the sun, it is night.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: When your part of Earth faces the sun, it is day. When your part turns away from the sun, it is night.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Earth turns slowly, round and round.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Earth turns slowly, round and round.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: In a small town near the sea, Samira woke up early.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: She thought about the whole big sky — the sun that made her shadow long and short, the clouds that floated by, the countless stars, and the gentle moon.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: She thought about the whole big sky — the sun that made her shadow long and short, the clouds that floated by, the countless stars, and the gentle moon.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: One cloud drifted over the sun, and for a moment her shadow disappeared. The sun was low again, and Samira's shadow grew long once more.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: One cloud drifted over the sun, and for a moment her shadow disappeared. The sun was low again, and Samira's shadow grew long once more.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: In the evening, the sky turned orange and red.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Why does the moon shine, Ayeeyo?” Samira asked.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Why does the moon shine, Ayeeyo?” Samira asked.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Samira had never really looked before. I never really looked up before.” Ayeeyo Selma smiled and held her hand.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Good people wonder about the world,” she said.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Our Earth is turning, round and round, all day long.” Samira thought about that. The whole Earth was turning, and she could not even feel it. “One, two, three, four…” But every time she looked, she found more. “Each star is like our sun, but very, very far away.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Noun. midday: The middle of the day, when the sun is highest. At midday my shadow is very short.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: evening. evening: The part of the day before night, when the sun sets. In the evening the sky turns orange.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: sunrise. sunrise: The time when the sun comes up in the morning. The sky is red at sunrise and at sunset.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the sun, moon, stars and clouds.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what a shadow is and how it forms.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about the sky in the morning, at midday and in the evening.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain in simple words why we have day and night.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use describing words like long, short, high and low.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the present simple and the past simple.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
